content:
With the HRT series being merged, we now have the option of getting rid of a pattern we used in Nova since the beginning, namely the constant passing of a &Bar0 to every method that needs to perform I/O and, to a lesser extend, the passing of a bound Device to do things like allocating memory.

Instead, we now can store references to these elements into each type that needs them, and access them from self, pretty much like what Nouveau does.

The most obvious candidate is Falcon. If we do this, method invocations like

falcon.dma_load(dev, bar, fw)

can be simplified into

falcon.dma_load(fw)

This makes a difference in the code: converting falcon to use this removes more than 50 LoCs in the codebase. On the other hand, Falcon gains a lifetime parameter, meaning that Falcon<Gsp> is now referred to as Falcon<'_, Gsp>.

To summarize:

Pros:

  • Less distracting arguments to methods,
  • Overall reduction of code,
  • That's probably how we would have done it since the beginning, had we had the
    option.

Cons:

  • Every structure gains a lifetime, both in their declaration and when referred
    to.

  • Structures get slightly bigger (although we are talking 16 bytes on average
    for a very limited number of instances, so that's really negligible?).

  • bar becomes self.bar in methods' code.

I think we should only apply these to top types like Falcon and Gsp - HALs which are only called locally can work efficiently with arguments. But IMHO the global readability improvement is a win.

Opinions? This sounds like a "good first issue", but since it is rather consequential for the direction of the project I'd like to see it done quickly if possible (but after merging Blackwell :slight_smile: ) if we decide this is the right way to proceed.

I can post a diff for Falcon somewhere if folks would like to see what it looks like in practice.

It is expected that lifetimes propagate through and I don't think it is a bad thing. I considered whether I want to send a patch series that does those changes, but also figured it would be a good first task for a new contributor.

In terms of where I would draw the line:

  • If a type only needs it in the constructor or a single method that is always called from a context where the device resource is already available, don't store the device resource in the type.
  • If a Device<Bound> is not needed, ARef<Device> can still be used.
How quickly are you talking about, a few weeks? I'd love to work on this now that I have a lot of free time and it's a good second issue :)

@Tim Kovalenko I'd say wait at least one week as we will try to land the (large) Blackwell series, then it should be doable without too many conflicts. This can also be done incrementally; Gsp would be a first good, smaller candidate, then you can look at other types that would benefit from it. Falcon is likely to be the largest, but also the one that will benefit the most.

If a Device<Bound> is not needed, ARef<Device> can still be used.

What would be the benefit, especially if we need to add a lifetime to the type because it stores the bar?
although I tend to agree that if we only need the Device for logging purposes, we should probably not expose more features that needed

Alexandre Courbot said:

Tim Kovalenko I'd say wait at least one week as we will try to land the (large) Blackwell series, then it should be doable without too many conflicts. This can also be done incrementally; Gsp would be a first good, smaller candidate, then you can look at other types that would benefit from it. Falcon is likely to be the largest, but also the one that will benefit the most.

Finally I can use my Blackwell card to test :) I'll start looking at the code and the places where I'll need to change it.
Once Blackwell is here, I'll start the incremental change.

Do you think it could be helpful for me or others to also review the Blackwell patch?

It has received some attention during the last few revisions (especially the first half, which I consider for merging very soon), but reviews (or fixes if the problem could not be flagged in time) are of course always welcome!

Hello @Tim Kovalenko, are you still planning to work on this? drm-rust-next should be in good shape now. If you are busy with other stuff it is also fine, just want to know if someone else should take this over. :slight_smile:

Alexandre Courbot said:

Hello Tim Kovalenko, are you still planning to work on this? drm-rust-next should be in good shape now. If you are busy with other stuff it is also fine, just want to know if someone else should take this over. :slight_smile:

Hey, I'm actually working on it right now. It's a bit slower as I have issues with rust-analyzer in the editor. Should be done soon :)
Sounds good! :thumbs_up: Thanks for following up
Actully, quick question, does rust analyzer usually have issue on your setup with type errors and especially trait implementation?
it's working well generally speaking, with the occasional loss of track in pin_init! blocks
I wrote a small nvim macro to kinda output compiler results into a trouble list but it's still a bit of a pain.

Alexandre Courbot said:

it's working well generally speaking, with the occasional loss of track in pin_init! blocks

huh, so maybe it's an issue with my setup as it works well within the file but sometimes it doesn't catch errors. I did this make LLVM=1 prepare rust-analyzer and it helped but not fully.
ah, now that you mention it some errors are indeed not always well reported by rust-analyzer
Might be intersting to take a look, as who knows, maybe I could figure something out after this task

Addressed the feedback and send a new version. After this is merged, I'll continue with other parts of the refactor. I'm moving this weekend, so I might not get much time until mid-next week.

Also, unrelated; do you know why b4 prep --auto-to-cc generates a bigger send list than V1 eventhough the files changed are the same? The same happened on my previous Patch. Could it be due to another patch being merged between V1 and V2?
Thanks, will take a look shortly!

re: --auto-to-cc, I think the RISC-V maintainers get added whenever riscv appears in a patch - and yours touches fields that carry this name. What I usually do is remove the RISC-V maintainers when they get added like that

Hey @Alexandre Courbot, I'm still spending a lot of time on moving, ie unpacking and stuff. I plan to continue working on the second patch soon ish. If it's needed before, please let me know and I'll find time sooner :slight_smile: Apologies I didn't update earlier
